Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd have these bursts where I'd watch a ton of videos and participate in discussions, but then I'd completely disappear for weeks. I wanted to be more organized, but between my extracurriculars and just feeling overwhelmed, I never really established a consistent pattern. I'd often find myself scrolling through the LMS late at night without a real plan.
Q2: My pattern was totally dependent on whatever crisis was happening that week. If I had a deadline looming or if I suddenly felt guilty about not doing anything, I'd cram. But regular, scheduled study times? That wasn't really my thing. I kept telling myself I'd start a proper routine next week, but next week never came.
Q3: Usually it was a notification or remembering that I hadn't checked in for a while and feeling this wave of anxiety about it. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which honestly made me feel worse. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more because they felt less intimidating than regular assignments.
Q4: For a brief period when we had those gamified activities, I was actually logging in pretty regularly because it felt more like a game than work. It became almost addictive to check my progress and see the little rewards. But that routine fell apart once my anxiety spiked about everything else I was behind on. It's like I could only maintain habits when things felt manageable.
Q5: The videos were my lifeline since I'm such a visual learner. I watched nearly three hours of video content because seeing concepts explained visually just clicks for me in a way reading never does. The discussion boards were also helpful because I could see how other students interpreted things, which sometimes made more sense than the lectures themselves.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and the actual assignments, which is why my completion rate was so terrible. I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, but the readings felt so dense and my anxiety would spike every time I opened them. It's like I could engage with the easy stuff but avoided anything that required real commitment.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if I could see immediate progress or if it didn't trigger my anxiety. The gamified tasks were perfect because they broke things into small, achievable pieces. If an activity felt too big or unclear about what success looked like, I'd just close the tab and tell myself I'd figure it out later.
Q8: Required activities felt like this massive weight on my chest that I kept avoiding, while optional ones felt almost relaxing because there was no pressure. I'd actually do optional discussion posts because they felt like a choice, but required assignments paralyzed me. It's backwards, I know, but the pressure of required work made me freeze up.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of materials to study offline because I don't have reliable internet at home. I'd screenshot slides, save PDFs to my phone, and review them during my commute or between my extracurricular activities. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer, just me and my notes.
Q10: So many times I was studying from printed notes or downloaded videos on my phone, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd spend hours going over visual diagrams I'd drawn based on the video content, trying to memorize concepts. The LMS only saw me when I was online, but I was often learning in coffee shops with no wifi.
Q11: Constantly. I'd watch a video once online, but then I'd have it saved and rewatch it multiple times offline. Same with slides and PDFs. My first viewing was just to download everything, and the real studying happened later when I could focus without worrying about my connection dropping.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just crashed for me emotionally. My anxiety was through the roof, I was juggling extracurriculars, and I started avoiding the course entirely because looking at my low completion rate made me feel like a failure. The more behind I got, the harder it was to even open the LMS without feeling sick.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was stressed or tired, the LMS became this symbol of everything I was failing at. I'd log in, see all the incomplete tasks, and just close it immediately. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars meant I was always choosing between things, and coursework usually lost because it felt the most overwhelming.
Q14: All the time. I'd open materials and just stare at them while my mind was somewhere else entirely, worrying about everything I hadn't done. I'd have videos playing but not actually absorbing anything because I was too anxious. My high watch time doesn't mean I was actually learning during all of it.
Q15: The assignments were my biggest avoidance. Even though I knew they were important—and apparently I could do them well when I actually tried, given my assignment scores—I kept putting them off because starting felt impossible. The anxiety of potentially failing made me avoid them until it was too late to even try.
Q16: Deadlines would either light a fire under me or completely shut me down. Sometimes a deadline approaching would finally get me to submit something, but other times I'd see a deadline pass and just give up on that assignment entirely. It was like once I missed one, I felt too ashamed to try catching up.
Q17: When I got good feedback on the few assignments I actually completed, I was genuinely surprised and it motivated me briefly. But then I'd remember all the ones I hadn't done and the good feeling would disappear. Feedback came too late to change my overall pattern because I was already so far behind.
Q18: The structure felt both helpful and overwhelming. Having everything laid out showed me exactly how much I wasn't doing, which fed my anxiety. But the gamified elements they added actually worked for me because they made the structure feel more playful and less like a judgment of my failures.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement so much easier because they felt achievable and almost fun. But the sheer volume of required assignments with no flexibility made things harder. If there had been more ways to demonstrate learning that didn't involve traditional assignments, I might have done better.
Q20: That's literally me. I was super active in discussions, watched tons of videos, logged in frequently, but my assignment completion was almost nothing. For me, it was anxiety—I could engage with low-stakes activities but the high-stakes submissions terrified me. Being present isn't the same as being productive.
Q21: I think some people are just more efficient or they study offline like I did. You can learn a lot from downloaded materials and never show up in the system. The LMS doesn't capture studying from notes, rewatching saved videos, or discussing concepts with friends in person.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're present and what you clicked on, but they can't show if you actually understood anything or if you were just anxiously scrolling. They miss all the offline work, the emotional state behind the numbers, and whether engagement was meaningful or just performative.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material and making an effort to understand it, not just going through the motions. For me, I was engaged in some ways—I genuinely found the content interesting—but my anxiety prevented that engagement from translating into completed work. Engagement is mental and emotional, not just behavioral.
Q24: The data wouldn't show that I actually cared about this course and wanted to succeed, or how much my anxiety held me back. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying offline, the panic attacks before deadlines, or how the gamified intervention actually made me feel hopeful for a while. Numbers can't capture how hard I was fighting against my own brain.
